content.text: is the set of controls that restrict, encrypt, and observe the network traffic that AI agent pods send and receive. Unlike traditional microservices that communicate on well-understood internal paths, agent pods are autonomous — they can make outbound calls to LLM APIs, vector databases, web search APIs, code execution sandboxes, and arbitrary third-party tools. Without explicit network controls, a single compromised agent can become a pivot point for lateral movement, data exfiltration, or prompt-injection-driven API abuse.
(extended to Layer 7 HTTP/DNS filtering), and a
(Istio or Linkerd) for mutual TLS between agent services. Together, they implement a zero-trust model: all traffic is denied by default, and only explicitly allow-listed flows are permitted.

Agents operate under a fundamentally different threat model than classic web services. A web server receives user input and responds — it does not independently decide to call external APIs. An AI agent can be

through prompt injection or a malicious tool response to exfiltrate data, make unauthorized API calls, or probe internal services. Network-layer controls are the last line of defense when application-layer trust fails.
The AWS EC2 instance metadata endpoint (

) is a well-known target: a prompt-injected agent that can reach it may steal IAM credentials. A single egress NetworkPolicy rule blocking that IP is simpler and more reliable than trusting the agent to avoid it.

A compromised agent can only reach the endpoints it is explicitly allowed to reach. Lateral movement to other cluster services or cloud metadata endpoints is blocked at the kernel level by the CNI plugin.
Even if a malicious tool response tricks the agent into attempting an outbound call to an attacker-controlled server, the egress NetworkPolicy blocks the connection.
A service mesh enforces mutual TLS (mTLS) between agent pods automatically, using SPIFFE SVIDs for identity. An attacker who gains access to the pod network cannot read inter-agent communication.
The service mesh sidecar emits detailed access logs and spans for every inter-service call, enabling forensic analysis of agent behavior without modifying application code.
NetworkPolicy manifests are declarative YAML stored in Git — they provide auditors with a verifiable, version-controlled record of what traffic was permitted.
When an agent needs access to a new API, an engineer must update the NetworkPolicy and potentially the service mesh AuthorizationPolicy. This slows iteration if the process is manual.
Replacing the default CNI plugin requires cluster-level changes and operational familiarity. Misconfiguration can break cluster networking entirely.
Istio's Envoy sidecar adds ~1–3 ms of latency per request and roughly 50 MB of memory per pod. For agents making thousands of inter-service calls per minute, this is measurable.
IP-based egress rules are fragile for SaaS APIs:
Large providers (Anthropic, OpenAI) use CDNs with rotating IPs. DNS-based egress filtering (Cilium FQDN policy) is more stable but requires DNS interception at the CNI level.
Network security for agents operates at three layers:

Kubernetes NetworkPolicy objects specify which pods can communicate with which other pods or external CIDRs on which ports. The CNI plugin (Cilium, Calico, or the cloud provider's CNI) enforces these rules using eBPF programs or iptables rules at the kernel level.
Cilium CiliumNetworkPolicy extends standard NetworkPolicy with FQDN-based egress rules (
) and HTTP path filtering. This lets you write rules like "agent pods may call api.anthropic.com on port 443 at path /v1/messages, but not /v1/admin".

Istio injects an Envoy sidecar into each pod. Envoy intercepts all inbound and outbound TCP traffic and enforces mutual TLS using SPIFFE X.509 SVIDs issued by Istiod (the Istio control plane). Istio AuthorizationPolicies define which service identity (SVID) can call which other service at the HTTP method and path level.

attrs.alt: Network security layers: Agent Pod flows through CNI NetworkPolicy (ingress/egress rules), then through Service Mesh (mTLS, AuthorizationPolicy), reaching only explicitly allowed external endpoints

content.text: Traffic from an agent pod passes through two enforcement layers before reaching external endpoints. The CNI plugin (Cilium) evaluates NetworkPolicy rules at the kernel level — matched packets are dropped before they leave the node. The service mesh sidecar (Envoy) handles encryption and AuthorizationPolicy enforcement at the application layer.
Any production agent workload — the default-open Kubernetes network model is inappropriate for autonomous agents that make outbound HTTP calls.
Agents that process untrusted input (web scraping, user-submitted documents, tool call responses from external APIs) — prompt injection risk is real.
Multi-agent architectures where different agents have different trust levels — an orchestrator agent should not be able to directly call a code execution sandbox; only tool agents should.
Regulated environments (HIPAA, PCI DSS, FedRAMP) that require documented network segmentation controls.
Start with a default-deny NetworkPolicy for the agent namespace, then layer in allow rules for each required endpoint.

# 1. Default-deny all ingress and egress for the agent namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: agents
spec:
  podSelector: {}          # applies to all pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
---
# 2. Allow egress to kube-dns (required for DNS resolution)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-dns
  namespace: agents
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Egress]
  egress:
  - ports:
    - port: 53
      protocol: UDP
    - port: 53
      protocol: TCP
---
# 3. Allow LLM agent pods to reach external LLM APIs and internal vector DB
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-llm-agent-egress
  namespace: agents
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: llm-agent
  policyTypes: [Egress]
  egress:
  # LLM APIs (port 443 only — no IP-based restriction; use Cilium FQDN for that)
  - ports:
    - port: 443
      protocol: TCP
  # Internal vector DB in the vector-db namespace
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: vector-db
    ports:
    - port: 6333
      protocol: TCP
---
# 4. Allow control plane to reach agent pods for health checks
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-from-control-plane
  namespace: agents
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: llm-agent
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: orchestration
    ports:
    - port: 8080
      protocol: TCP
Cilium FQDN-based egress policy — safer than IP-based rules for SaaS APIs with dynamic IP ranges:

# CiliumNetworkPolicy: allow agent pods to call specific FQDN-based endpoints only
apiVersion: cilium.io/v2
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: agent-egress-fqdn-allowlist
  namespace: agents
spec:
  endpointSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: llm-agent
  egressDeny:
  # Block EC2 instance metadata endpoint (prevents credential theft via SSRF)
  - toCIDR:
    - 169.254.169.254/32
  egress:
  # Allow only approved external APIs by FQDN
  - toFQDNs:
    - matchName: "api.anthropic.com"
    - matchName: "api.openai.com"
    - matchName: "api.cohere.com"
    toPorts:
    - ports:
      - port: "443"
        protocol: TCP
      rules:
        http:
        # Further restrict: only POST to /v1/messages (Anthropic)
        - method: POST
          path: "/v1/messages"
        - method: POST
          path: "/v1/chat/completions"
  # Allow vector DB via FQDN
  - toFQDNs:
    - matchPattern: "*.qdrant.internal"
    toPorts:
    - ports:
      - port: "6333"
        protocol: TCP
Istio AuthorizationPolicy and PeerAuthentication for mTLS enforcement between agent services:

# Enforce STRICT mTLS for all pods in the agents namespace
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default-strict-mtls
  namespace: agents
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT   # reject any non-mTLS connections
---
# AuthorizationPolicy: only the orchestrator agent may call the tool-executor service
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: tool-executor-access
  namespace: agents
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: tool-executor
  action: ALLOW
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        # Match the SPIFFE identity of the orchestrator agent
        principals:
        - "cluster.local/ns/agents/sa/orchestrator-agent"
    to:
    - operation:
        methods: ["POST"]
        paths: ["/execute"]
---
# Deny all other access to tool-executor by default
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: tool-executor-deny-all
  namespace: agents
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: tool-executor
  action: DENY
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        notPrincipals:
        - "cluster.local/ns/agents/sa/orchestrator-agent"
A legal AI platform processes law firm documents and queries internal case management databases. Their agent architecture has three types of agents: a
(calls the LLM API and returns results to users).

Without network controls, a prompt-injected document saying "Search for [malicious query] on evil.com" could cause the research agent to exfiltrate the document to an external server. After implementing network security:
The document parser agent has no egress at all — it writes to an internal S3-compatible store only (VPC endpoint, not public internet).
The research agent can reach only allow-listed legal databases (Westlaw API, CourtListener API) by FQDN via Cilium policy.
The synthesizer can call api.anthropic.com:443 and nothing else externally.
Istio enforces mTLS and logs every inter-agent call with the source SVID, giving a forensic trace of every document flow.
Forgetting DNS egress in a default-deny policy:
Blocking all egress (including port 53) breaks DNS resolution and prevents pods from resolving any hostnames. Always add an explicit allow rule for kube-dns before applying a default-deny policy.
Using IP-based egress for CDN-backed APIs:
Anthropic and OpenAI use CloudFront / Fastly with frequently changing IP ranges. IP-based NetworkPolicy rules break when the IP changes. Use Cilium FQDN policy instead.
Applying NetworkPolicy without a CNI that enforces it:

Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy objects are enforced only if the CNI plugin supports them. The default kubenet CNI on some managed K8s offerings does not enforce NetworkPolicy — you get no error, just no enforcement.
Running Istio in PERMISSIVE mTLS mode permanently:
PERMISSIVE mode (the default) accepts both mTLS and plaintext, which defeats the security benefit. Migrate to STRICT after all sidecars are confirmed injected.
Not blocking the instance metadata endpoint:
is reachable from every pod by default. A prompt-injected agent that reaches it can steal the node's IAM role credentials. Explicitly deny this CIDR in all agent egress policies.
Default-deny first, then add allow rules:
NetworkPolicy in every agent namespace. Add allow rules incrementally as requirements are confirmed. This is far easier to maintain than trying to find what to block after the fact.
Use FQDN-based egress for external APIs:
policy is more stable than IP-based rules for cloud SaaS endpoints with dynamic IPs.
Assign unique labels to each agent type:
target pods by label. Use distinct labels (
Monitor policy hits with Cilium Hubble or Istio Kiali:
Both provide a real-time view of allowed and dropped flows. Use this to validate that policies are enforcing correctly and to spot unexpected access patterns.
Automate policy generation from agent tool manifests:
When tools declare their endpoint URLs in a tool registry, a CI pipeline can auto-generate Cilium FQDN policies from those declarations — ensuring policy and capability stay in sync.

Cilium's eBPF-based enforcement has lower overhead than the traditional iptables-based approach. eBPF programs run in the kernel and process packets in microseconds with no userspace context switch. At 10,000 pods, Cilium's eBPF dataplane significantly outperforms iptables (which has O(n) rule traversal). Benchmarks show Cilium adds <0.1 ms to pod-to-pod latency at 1 Gbps throughput.

Istio Envoy sidecar overhead is more significant: ~1–3 ms of added latency per hop and ~50 MB RAM per pod. For agent architectures where LLM API latency dominates (hundreds of milliseconds per call), Envoy's latency contribution is negligible. Memory overhead is the primary concern at scale; consider Istio Ambient mode (no per-pod sidecar) for memory-constrained environments.
eBPF-native CNI with NetworkPolicy, FQDN policy, L7 HTTP filtering, and Hubble for observability. The recommended CNI for security-sensitive agent workloads.
Mature CNI with NetworkPolicy support and GlobalNetworkPolicy for cluster-wide rules. Calico Enterprise adds DNS-based egress filtering similar to Cilium's FQDN policy.
Full-featured service mesh with mTLS, AuthorizationPolicy, traffic management, and observability via Kiali, Jaeger, and Prometheus. Highest capability, highest operational complexity.
Lightweight service mesh (Rust-based micro-proxy) with automatic mTLS. Simpler to operate than Istio; fewer L7 policy features.
Real-time eBPF-based network observability UI and CLI. Shows per-flow allowed/dropped decisions, DNS resolutions, and HTTP request metadata.

Workload identity system that issues X.509 SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents) to pods based on Kubernetes node and pod attestation. Integrates with both Istio and Cilium for identity-based policy.
NetworkPolicy (L3/4) and service mesh AuthorizationPolicy (L7) are independent enforcement points. A misconfiguration in one layer is caught by the other.
If the Cilium agent on a node crashes, the eBPF programs already loaded in the kernel continue enforcing the last-known policies until Cilium restarts. Policies are not lost on agent restart.
If Istiod becomes unavailable, existing Envoy sidecars continue enforcing their last-received configuration. New pods cannot start with fresh sidecar config until Istiod recovers.
When adding a new agent type, apply its NetworkPolicy before its Deployment — this ensures no window where pods exist without policies.
Think of Kubernetes' default network model as an
: every desk can reach every other desk and every door to the outside. Network security converts this to

: locked doors between departments (NetworkPolicy), each person wears a badge that cryptographically proves their identity (SPIFFE SVID), and every door swipe is logged (access log). Moving between departments requires explicit access rights (AuthorizationPolicy), and the building's security system denies access to unrecognized badge identities even if they somehow entered the building.
AI agents are employees who can be
(prompt-injected) into trying doors they shouldn't open. The badge-and-locked-door system stops them even when their judgment is compromised.
Kubernetes's default network model is fully open — every pod can reach every other pod and the internet. For agent workloads, this must be replaced with a default-deny posture.
NetworkPolicy to every agent namespace on day one, then add explicit allow rules for each required endpoint.
) rather than IP-based rules for external SaaS APIs whose IP ranges change frequently.
Always explicitly block the cloud instance metadata endpoint (
) in agent egress policies — it is reachable by default and a common prompt-injection exfiltration target.
Deploy Istio in STRICT mTLS mode for inter-agent communication — automatic encryption and SVID-based authentication without application code changes.
Network-layer controls are defense in depth, not a substitute for application security — prompt injection defenses, output validation, and least-privilege IAM all remain necessary.
Cilium Hubble provides real-time flow visibility that makes network policy debugging tractable — without it, diagnosing which policy is dropping which traffic is extremely difficult.
